data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Neutral Sentiment Grows
data.description:
Neutral sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 3.1 percentage points to 25.4%. Bullish sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 14th time in 16 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 5.2 percentage points to 17.7%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 39th time in 41 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 2.1 percentage points to 56.9%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 20th time in 22 weeks. Bearish sentiment has now been above 50% for eight consecutive weeks, the longest period over 50% in the survey's history.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 1.0 percentage points to –31.4%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 15th time in 17 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what types of stocks they are favoring.

Here is how they responded:

  • Dividend stocks: 35.5%
  • Value stocks: 15.8%
  • Growth stocks: 9.6%
  • Small-cap stocks: 2.5%
  • A mix of the above/other: 36.3%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 25.4%, down 3.1 percentage points
  • Neutral: 17.7%, up 5.2 percentage points
  • Bearish: 56.9%, down 2.1 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Bank OZK: Still A Steal Post-Q1 Results
data.description:
In a quasi-case of Groundhog Day, shares of Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) have been struggling lately, falling by around 11% since my last piece in December. I say a quasi-case: OZK has actually outperformed regional bank ETFs in that time, albeit it has had a harder fall from its year-to-date peak in February.

Followers of OZK will not be surprised. This Arkansas-based lender writes a relatively large amount of construction loans, and given recent events, well, it wouldn't be hard to craft a downbeat narrative for OZK compared to the average bank.

I continue to remain much more bullish. For one, OZK has more than proved its spurs over the years in terms of credit quality. Not just that, but the stock now trades for less than 7x forward EPS, affording investors a very large margin of safety. Given first quarter results look fine to me, I'm leaving my 'Strong Buy' rating for OZK in place.

Q1 2025 Review

In the first quarter, OZK earned $167.9 million in net income, or $1.47 per share. The latter was down 2.6% year-on-year and 5.8% sequentially, but still mapped to a stellar 1.77% return on average assets.

Net interest income was relatively flat at $374.5 million, with OZK's net interest margin declining 2bps sequentially to 4.31%. OZK's loan book is largely variable rate, and loans account for the vast majority of its interest-earning assets. Following last year's rate cuts, it has simply lost a bit more on the asset side than it has gained from cheaper funding.

Positively, NIM contraction is moderating. Because OZK doesn't source particularly cheap funding, then if interest rates hold steady, it should benefit quite nicely from the repricing of its more market-like liabilities (like time deposits, for example). This should lead to NIM expansion later in the year.

Operating costs rose ~10% year-on-year to $147 million, exceeding revenue growth. Despite this, OZK remains an incredibly efficient bank. Expressed relative to assets, it spends much less than the average peer does on operating costs. This is also true once you strip out non-interest income as well.

Underwriting is really the strongest aspect of Bank OZK. In short, it writes relatively high-yielding loans while suffering very few loan losses. Over the past seventeen years, OZK's average gross loan yield was over 100bps higher than the peer average, while its average net charge-off ratio was around 30bps below the average.

Looking at its balance sheet, loans account for a higher share of OZK's assets than the average bank. Keeping losses contained is therefore relatively more important in terms of its profitability. Alongside the nature of its lending (more commercial real estate, particularly construction), this could also explain why the market tends to punish OZK more during periods of economic uncertainty.

With the above in mind, you could also argue that monitoring credit quality is more important here than for other lenders. So far, things still look pretty good on that front. Net charge-offs did increase 5bps compared to FY2024, but at 25bps, losses remain low. Only $45 million of OZK's loans are past due, equal to just 0.14% of total lending. Nonperforming assets were around 0.55%, roughly flat quarter-on-quarter. This is mostly foreclosed real estate that OZK can claw back value from. Plus, its ratio of NPAs to total assets is also not worse than other banks.

Given the recent bout of uncertainty, it is understandable that investors may be worried about credit risk. OZK has several lines of defense here. Should defaults rise, well, its average loan-to-cost and loan-to-value ratios were only 45% last quarter in its RESG division. That affords it a pretty solid collateral cushion. Furthermore, OZK is generating a very strong ~2.75% pre-provision ROA. That is much, much higher than the average bank, meaning OZK simply has more scope to absorb credit charges. It has a $639 million credit loss allowance, equal to 1.28% of loans and unfunded commitments. Relative to actual NPAs, this is equal to a strong ~3x cover. Thirdly, OZK has ample capital. Its ratio of tangible common equity to tangible assets was 12.55% at the end of last quarter. While we obviously don't have comprehensive peer data yet, that will be well above average.

The Valuation

OZK stock trades for $41.11 as I type, equal to a little under tangible book value per share. This is a bank that earned a strong 14.3% Return on Average Tangible Common Equity [ROATCE] last quarter, meaning its P/E ratio is well into the single-digits. OZK could earn a 10% ROATCE and still trade for less than 10x earnings. Looking at its actual historical ROATCE (around 15% over the past decade), that seems conservative to say the least.

Given its strong earnings and modest dividend payout ratio, OZK has continued to compound its TBVPS at a strong clip. Last quarter, this line rose nearly 13% year-on-year to $42.48. As you can see below, that is pretty much right in line with its historical rate. Looking at consensus earnings estimates, OZK should continue to grow its balance sheet at a double-digit annualized clip in the future as well.

Finally, OZK's dividend now yields around 4.4%, and that is on a payout ratio of just 25% of net income. Between balance sheet growth, P/TBVPS expansion and a nice dividend yield, the stock continues to offer investors excellent medium-term total returns potential.

Conclusion

There wasn't much in Bank OZK's first quarter results that caused me concern. NII and margin dynamics were already telegraphed beforehand, and besides, the bank is still earning a stellar ROA. OZK being OZK, credit quality is always going to be in the spotlight: such is the nature of its business model. Positively, things still look very good on that front.

As for the stock, while multiple expansion may well require a shift in economic sentiment to occur, a compelling combination of growth and yield can still drive very good returns either way. On that basis, I am leaving my 'Strong Buy' rating in place.

data.title: JPMorgan: Well-Positioned To Withstand Any 'Kerfuffle'
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE:JPM), published in January 2025, I dissected the company’s fourth quarter performance and analysed the long-term impact of the company’s ambitious plan of expanding its consumer business into Europe. I had a HOLD rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, JPM has declined 10.7%, in line with the S&P 500’s decline, which was 11.3% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the company’s first quarter earnings report and examine whether JPM has the necessary tools to withstand a potential recession, especially since the odds of one has increased in recent week.

First Quarter Highlights

JPM had a very strong start to the year, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. Q1 revenues came in at $45.31 billion, up 8.05% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $1.21 billion. Diluted EPS came in at $5.07, up 14.2% y/y and comfortably beating analyst estimates by $0.43. The bank’s book value per share came in at $119.24, up 12% y/y, and tangible book value per share came in at $100.36, up 13% y/y. Net interest income, a key metrics for the bank, came in at $22.6 billion. Provisions for credit losses rose slightly, coming in at $3.31 billion, and missed analyst estimates of $2.70 billion. Return on equity for the first quarter came in at 18%, comfortably beating analyst estimates of 16.2%, and return on tangible equity (ROTCE) came in at 21%, the same as the previous quarter.

The company reaffirmed its 2025 guidance for net interest income (excl. markets), expected to be approximately $90 billion. The bank now expects overall net interest income to come in at approximately $94.5 billion, a slight uptick from the previous guidance of $94 billion, which was also what the consensus estimate was.

JPM Demonstrates Resilience Despite The Tariff-Induced “Kerfuffle”

I enjoy JPM CEO Jamie Dimon’s interviews and commentary, especially given his penchant for colourful ways to describe the economy, policies and regulation amongst other things. During the first quarter earnings call, he described the trade war induced panic that was seen in the Treasury markets as a ‘kerfuffle.’

There is, however, no evidence of any ‘kerfuffle’ in JPM itself if Q1 results are to go by, despite the macroeconomic uncertainties becoming challenging with each passing day. Every single segment of the company performed remarkably well, despite the uncertainty. What was striking to me was the growth seen in the Consumer & Community Banking (CCB) Division. The y/y growth may have only been 3.7%, but it is a remarkable jump compared to the previous quarters. For instance, the growth stood at 1.5% y/y in Q4 of FY24, -3.1% y/y in the third quarter, and 2.7% y/y in the second quarter. Given that the macroenvironment was even more challenging in the quarter gone by, compared to the previous quarter, the fact that JPM was able to achieve this growth, that too with a high base effect, demonstrates the company’s resilience and proves why investors should look at this name, especially during turbulent times like what we are facing now. The bank also announced that US consumer continued to be resilient despite falling sentiment, as reflected by the 7% y/y increase in debit and credit card sales volume. The growth was a lot higher than its consumer-focused peer, BAC, which saw credit- and debit-card spending rising 4% y/y. JPM, however, did warn that the higher growth could be due to consumers “front-loading” their spending ahead of a potential increase in prices due to tariffs. Nonetheless, the CCB division’s growth and performance is a testament to the bank’s ability to weather a challenging macroenvironment in my opinion.

There was also continued strength seen in both the bank’s Corporate & Investment Banking [cib] Division and its Asset & Wealth Management (AWM) Division, both of which, registered a second consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, although growth across both segments did show a decline. On a sequential basis, however, the CIB division registered a double-digit growth for the first time in five quarters. The bank also saw a 12% y/y jump in investment banking fees and maintained its number 1 ranking with a wallet share of 9%. The bank was also able to take full advantage of the increased volatility seen in equity markets ever since President Trump came into the White House, as demonstrated by a 48% y/y jump in equity trading revenue.

Overall, if there’s one key takeaway from JPM’s first quarter, it is that the bank continues to be resilient to the challenging macroenvironment. Despite an elevated net charge-off rate, the bank was still able to demonstrate impressive growth in the CCB division, and both the CIB and AWM divisions continued to be strong catalysts. What is even more impressive is that the bank has already made substantial provisions for credit losses, as the threat of a recession continues to remain elevated albeit lower than during the height of the tariff wars. This means that the bank, in my opinion, remains in a much better position should the recession fears materialize.

Furthermore, President Trump does seem to listen to Jamie Dimon, as evidenced by his recent admission of Mr. Dimon playing a role in his decision to put a 90-day pause on his reciprocal tariffs. Should this be the case for the future, then based on the Mr. Dimon's commentary on the need for more deregulation, the odds of this happening have certainly increased in my opinion. Should this materialize, then it would be an additional tailwind to JPM’s growth. Today, in my opinion, investors need to be looking out for high-quality names to take cover as the macroenvironment becomes more and more uncertain. Based on its Q1 performance and the outlook, JPM certainly fits the bill.

Valuation

Forward P/B Approach

Price Target

$271.00

Projected Forward P/B Multiple

1.83

Projected FY25 Book Value Per Share

$148.15

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv) and Author’s Calculations

According to LSEG Data, the company currently trades at a forward P/B of 1.83, making it the most expensive among its peers. It is also more expensive today relative to its historical levels, with its historical forward P/B median multiple being 1.5x.

In my last article on JPM, I made my case on why JPM deserves a re-rating of its multiple, given the continued strength and resilience across all its segments. Despite tariff-related uncertainties, and despite the bank’s IB division expected to remain under pressure at least until there’s clarity on the current Administration’s tariff policies, the bank still re-affirmed its FY25 guidance. Furthermore, given that it is a more well-rounded bank compared to some of its peers such as GS and MS, it deserves a multiple higher than them. As such, I have assumed a forward P/B multiple of 1.83x for my calculations, slightly lower than my previous estimate of 2.03x.

JPM’s book value of equity at the end of the first quarter stood at $351.42 billion. The company re-affirmed its NII guidance at $94 billion, which to me is a reasonable estimate even after accounting for the tariff-related uncertainties. After accounting for a tax rate of 20.45%, the NII after taxes come in at $74.78 billion. The company announced a 12% y/y increase in dividends for the first quarter, which is in line with its 10-year dividend growth rate of 11.42%. Assuming the company maintains a 12% y/y dividend growth and assuming the total number of shares outstanding at 2.78 billion, the total dividends for FY25 are expected to come in $14.32 billion. Based on the same number of shares outstanding, the projected book value per share for FY25 is then projected to come in at $148.15 [(351.42 + 74.78 – 14.32)/2.78], representing a y/y growth of about 28% y/y.

A forward P/B of 1.83 and a projected FY25 book value of share of $148.15 results in a price target of $271, which represents an upside of about 18% from current levels. My price target is higher than my previous one ($217), since I calculated my estimate of the bank’s book value per share rather than relying on the company’s track record of historical surprise to offer a more appropriate estimate.

Furthermore, despite the recent selloff, JPM continues to enjoy strong overall momentum, reflected by the B+ grade on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum Scale. Even its short-term momentum stands at a respectable B-. Given the considerable upside from current levels and given the strong momentum seen in the stock, I am upgrading my rating on the stock from a HOLD to a BUY.

Risk Factors

The main risk factor to my bull case is that the recession does arrive a lot earlier than expected and more importantly, it stays for longer. JPM’s own economists, after all, themselves raised the probability of a 2025 recession to 60% from 40%. While there is no doubt that JPM’s balance sheet remains strong, it still would take a hit given that it would have to substantially increase its provision for credit losses, given the increasing delinquencies that would come with the recession. The credit card net charge-off rate has been steadily increasing in the last three quarters, albeit the growth has been marginal. In a recessionary environment, the growth rate would no longer be marginal, which would have a significant negative impact on the bank’s bottom-line.

The second risk factor to my bull case is if the M&A environment remains subdued for a lot longer than initially anticipated. There was a lot of anticipation that deal-making environment would get a boost under a new Administration, but so far, this is yet to materialize. The expectation now is that deal activity picks up in the second half of FY25 once the environment becomes a lot more deregulatory in nature. This is far from guaranteed, given that for now, the FTC has announced it would continue to maintain the Biden-era merger guidelines. JPM’s management, during the earnings call, also cited the need for the uncertainty to be substantially reduced before deal activity can pick up. As such, if uncertainties do remain elevated, then the Investment Banking division of the bank would be negatively affected.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another strong quarterly performance by JPM, with the banking giant comfortably beating both the top- and bottom-line estimates. Despite a challenging macroenvironment, the bank’s CCB division demonstrated strong resilience. Furthermore, both the CIB and AWM divisions registered yet another quarter of strong double-digit growth. Overall, it was an all-round performance from JPM and the fact that the bank re-affirmed its guidance despite increasing its provisions for loan losses shows that it has the capability to withstand the recessionary storm, should we get one.

From a valuation perspective, the recent selloff seen in the stock has made it attractive, and as such, I am upgrading my rating on the stock from a HOLD to a BUY. There might be “hurricanes” and “kerfuffles” occurring in the US now as the trade war with China rages on. Based on current evidence, however, JPM remains immune to them.

data.title: Lynas Rare Earths: A Cyclical Play
data.description:
Lynas Rare Earths Limited (OTCPK:LYSCF)/(OTCPK:LYSDY) is a unique opportunity to ride a cyclical wave driven by China's tightening of rare earth minerals being supplied to the U.S. are part of a broader response to the tariffs of the Trump Administration.

I believe Lynas is uniquely positioned to benefit from rare earth price increases from this constrained supply, with a balance sheet that doesn't create a lot of risk if commodity prices turn sour. For that reason, I think it's a solid Buy.

Lynas's Position in Rare Earths

The way Lynas has positioned itself is unique among non-Chinese companies. As the PBS report I linked in the intro mentioned, 70% of America's rare earth minerals are imported from China. A supply constraint of this magnitude necessarily puts upward pressure on these commodity prices. Speaking in the Q2 FY 2025 earnings call, CEO Amanda Lacaze:

The great control of feedstock, generally, we would believe favors Lynas as we are a proven separator with proven resources, and we remain the only non-Chinese producer who can bank the full value of any improvements in the oxide prices.

Rare earths are technically in many places, but the tricky part is having the facilities already set up to mine and process those minerals.

Lynas already has such three such sites, two in Australia (their home country), and one in Malaysia.

They aren't a speculative rare earths project, with annual production of 12K to 15K tonnes of minerals in recent years. As such, they are a viable alternative if Chinese sources tighten.

Financial History and Condition

One may note that the image I just shared indicates that, while volume didn't shift too much over the years, total revenues did, as their average selling price shot up along these cyclical lines. Cost of sales were also relatively stable, allowing Lynas to capture this upside.

Taking a broader view, we see how favorable prices showcase the kind of operating leverage that Lynas has. (Seeking Alpha data in USD.)

Looking at it from a cash flow perspective, we cash from operation skyrockets in these scenarios. Much of this was spent on capex to build out the very capacity that was mentioned before.

Above, they reported that they ended the quarter with $308M in cash. This gives substantial coverage against their debt position.

Between the short-term and long-term borrowings, this sufficiently covers their total debt of $171M. According to their latest quarterly report, this all comes from a loan facility that matures in June 2030, indicating that they have a lot of flexibility on the balance sheet that minimizes much of the risk in case cycles don't favor them.

The Buy Case

Ultimately, this idea is a very simple cyclical play. When prices are up, Lynas benefits, and while recent operating cash flows are in tens of millions per year (in USD), their operating leverage jumps this up to several multiples. As this would increase earnings, there's the moment for shareholders to benefit.

Shares peaked near $8 when commodity prices were up and boosted their revenues. With more capacity and potentially a much sharper impact to rare earth markets ahead, I think there's room for a similar double in the foreseeable future.

There are some risks that are worth highlighting, however:

  • Trump and China backing down
  • Potential moves against Australia
  • Currency exchange risks
  • OTC trading

The first is simple. How long Trump's tariff moves will last (whether they are policy or a tactic in a long-term negotiation) is uncertain. If Trump and China and back down and return to normalcy, which could technically happen at any time, that takes away the supply shortage that would increase prices of rare earths.

In a similar vein, China is not the only target of tariffs, and the same could happen to Australia. If sale of minerals to the U.S. were limited or hindered, this also hurts the thesis.

Even if those issues don't present themselves, the company discloses that some risk exists in relation to the currency of all three countries (see exchange losses recorded above). While most readers will be directly affected by the relationship between the USD and the AUD, even the Chinese Renminbi indirectly matters because of China's influence on mineral prices, and so exchange rates among all three are key.

Lastly, as this is a foreign stock, it trades OTC in the U.S. With a cyclical play like this, it helps to have an exit plan, as OTC stocks can be constrained by insufficient liquidity. American investors should figure out their exit plan before buying into this company.

Conclusion

It's not complicated. If China closes the lid on rare earths, even just for spite, American customers have to look elsewhere. As it stands, Lynas is the lone, reasonable alternative that is completely outside of China. While there are external factors that could confound this, internally Lynas has made all the right moves in preparing its capacity for such a moment.

With the history of its positive results when commodity prices favor them and the impact on the stock price, I think it's easy to see what happens as China's policy on restricting rare earths goes into place and constrains supply. For that reason, I feel comfortable rating this stock a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CEFS: A 'Non-Discounted' Take On Discounted CEFs (In A Bear Market)
data.description:
I've worked with several funds, and arbitrage is always a hot topic, often framed negatively, almost like an elite strategy that has become less and less accessible. Personally, I don't think that's true, it's just about knowing where to look. According to Boaz Weinstein, the creator of the Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (BATS:CEFS), closed-end funds "are one of the last places where inefficiencies still exist."

CEFS is a well-known fund, both for its arbitrage strategies and, in part, for its hefty expense ratio of 4.29%, a number that sounds scary just to write. And yet, experience has taught me that costs should only be evaluated after examining the other aspects of the fund. So that's exactly what I'll do here.

But first: CEFS, here are the fund features you need to know

I'll start with the strategy, definitely the most interesting aspect, as it's an actively managed fund of funds offered as an ETF. CEFS buys CEFs (excuse the wordplay) with large NAV discounts, aiming for the discount to shrink. Then they pressure the manager through activist strategies, with the goal of closing the discount. And this is theoretically CEFS's competitive advantage, and this premium over the market should (theoretically) justify such a high management fee.

As of today: According to the prospectus, at least 80% of the capital should be invested in CEFs, although it maintains wide freedom of choice, including derivatives, shorts, and leverage. And that's exactly the case: it holds about 97 CEFs in the portfolio, with a weighted average discount of 8-9%, up to 6% leverage, and performs active rate hedging.

Currently, it holds activist positions in ECAT (BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Trust), with over 12% of NAV; and second BTZ (BlackRock Tech & Private Equity), nearly 9%. The first has a NAV discount of 5%, the second a NAV discount of 6.12%.

This leads to an annualized yield of 7.72%, a 30-day SEC yield of 9.30%, distributed in part monthly.

The flexibility is also seen in "unusual" holdings: such as ASA (over 7%), gold and precious metals, like PDI (4.7%) for the income section, and GDV (5.3%) for the dividend section. Small details, but they highlight not only a focus on NAV discount strategies, but also a certain diversification. Let's not forget that this is a fund of funds, this should (at least theoretically) level out standard deviation, shifting the attention to the income side of CEFs.

But does this hold up in reality?

Am I being too simplistic if I answer this by comparing it to the S&P 500? Maybe a bit, but I don't know of any other actively managed ETFs that follow an activist strategy to compare it with. So, I'll start by saying that over the last 5 years, it's shown a very competitive performance compared to the S&P 500, with a standard deviation of 11.29, which is lower. That translates into a better Sharpe ratio.

Its total return is therefore very correlated with U.S. equities, which in my view makes CEFS not a diversifying tool for a portfolio, even if it's often associated with that role. In this regard, the comparisons made by Boaz Weinstein himself, like here, are always interesting: he uses other funds as benchmarks, showing that while CEFS is more expensive, it also tends to outperform, and that activism is complex, but brings decisive alpha for this category.

In this sense, I find this table very useful, as it shows how CEFS, a fund characterized by activism and higher costs, has indeed outperformed its peers focused only on NAV arbitrage.

How to approach these funds

There's no rule here, rather, a personal opinion. One element I personally value a lot in managing my finances (and one that often causes disagreements with colleagues when discussing portfolio management) is a broad use of DCA, especially during a low phase like the current one.

When it comes to income funds, for me, there's no strategy that can compete with a long-term DCA implementation based on a minimum signal (30 points) on the 14-period RSI on a weekly timeframe, combined with dividend reinvestment.

This would generate entry points like those summarized in this simulation, I created using a program I built to send me the right alerts.

Risk

I could keep it simple and say that CEFS shares the same risks as any other U.S. stock, given its correlation; that would be graphically accurate, but not thorough, especially for a fund with such high fees that remains competitive likely because it works well with NAV discount arbitrage.

What widens when trader anxiety or market tension rises? The so-called "risk wrong way." This is often associated with bear markets, but I personally prefer to link it to phases of high uncertainty (technically, zones with a high VIX). When uncertainty rises, the gap between NAV and market price could potentially widen due to typical supply and demand mechanisms. In this sense, panic selling becomes particularly harmful, an eventuality whose probability increases when isolating cases where the VIX is above 30 points, like now.

Given that CEFS has 80% exposure to CEFs and maintains 6% leverage, the potential risk could exceed that of the S&P 500. I say "potential" because active management (theoretically) should also be able to cushion losses better than the S&P 500, which is a static index in this regard.

In fact, in my experience, it's precisely in those moments when the tail end of the VIX spikes to extremes that you find out whether a fund like this is well managed, just one wrong call can create a performance gap against the broader market that becomes virtually irrecoverable.

Conclusion

Following the core strategy of DCA combined with dividend reinvestment, we're currently in a low zone, one that could continue to decline, because mathematical precision and statistical certainty simply don't exist in markets. However, considering that this fund has historically demonstrated excellent management, I don't see any technical reason not to remain positive on CEFS.

Of course, the "risk wrong way" is currently elevated, so I believe it's important to factor in that the probability of further declines is higher than in previous RSI lows. That said, the whole logic behind DCA is precisely to protect against such risks.

data.title: Gold, Guns, And The Market: What Rising Tensions Really Mean For Your Portfolio
data.description:
I’ve been bullish on gold for over a year now. That doesn’t make me a gold bug. I’m not waving a “repent now” placard on a street corner, and I’m certainly not forecasting mushroom clouds and Mad Max. I’m an investor—an ex-trader—who believes in the oldest trading maxim: trade what you see, not what you think.

A year ago, I saw gold getting ready to move. Not because I had a bunker full of beans and bullion, but because the charts were whispering—and then shouting—that something was afoot. When markets speak, I listen. So, I started asking why gold was perking up.

Forget the fairy tale about governments someday going back to a gold standard. That ship sank with Nixon and isn’t coming back. However, central banks still hoard gold like it’s sacred. Why?

Here’s the answer that stuck: gold is the currency of war.

It has use cases, sure: electronics, jewellery, reserve diversification—but its strategic utility is what counts now. In a world of rising geopolitical strain, gold becomes the fallback. When the chips are down, it’s not crypto or fiat; it’s gold.

So, what are we seeing now?

Not Armageddon. But the pressure is rising. The ratchet is tightening. The system’s not breaking, but the creaks are getting louder.

Look at the United States. Its sudden industrial policy pivots aren’t random noise. The CHIPS Act, the push for domestic steel, the re-onshoring of manufacturing, the new buzz around shipbuilding—these aren’t isolated quirks. When you connect the dots, a pattern emerges: the U.S. is quietly (or not so quietly) positioning itself for potential conflict with China.

Now, that doesn’t mean war is imminent. Deterrence is a posture, not a plan. The best way to avoid war, historically, has been to be ready for it. That’s what we may be seeing: the U.S. gearing up not to fight, but to be taken seriously if it has to be.

That means rebuilding industrial muscle—because you can’t win a 21st-century conflict with PowerPoint presentations and imported semiconductors. You need steel, ships, chips, AI, and energy independence. Right now, America is strong militarily but hollowed out industrially. That’s changing.

This isn’t fate. It’s a thesis. And it’s tradable.

Because if this trend continues—if tension ratchets further—markets will reflect it long before the headlines do. That’s where gold comes in. It’s not about apocalypse. It’s about hedging tension.

And it’s not just gold. Rising tension affects everything. Some sectors thrive. Others get crushed.

Likely Outperformers:

  • Defence
  • Steel
  • Oil and Energy
  • Shipping
  • Chemicals
  • Agriculture and Food

Likely Underperformers:

  • Luxury goods
  • Travel
  • Consumer discretionary
  • Real estate
  • Entertainment

Think in pairs and hedge accordingly.

Take LVMH. I said in my article of March 26th that it looked like a canary in the coal mine—high on the globalisation pyramid, vulnerable to tariffs, recession, and geopolitical heat. Here’s the chart from then.

Here’s the chart now.

Uncomfortable accuracy.

The worst part? U.S. markets are still priced for perfection. Valuations remain near historic highs. If we do get turbulence, the drop could be sharp and long.

Gold, on the other hand, is already calling it. It’s telling us the mood music has changed. This doesn’t mean disaster is baked in. There are plenty of off-ramps. If things de-escalate, gold will drop—and I’ll be glad. Peace is more profitable than conflict.

But for now, I’m trading what I see, and what I see is a market that’s beginning to price in an increasingly dangerous world.

data.title: Northrop Grumman Q1 Preview: A Hedge Amid Global Restructuring (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC), the stock has gained ~5% in price, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has lost ~10% in price.

In a world currently being reshaped by geopolitical ambitions, Northrop Grumman is most aptly described as a stalwart portfolio hedge; it certainly isn't a hyper-growth story like some burgeoning AI defense startups. However, record order backlogs, driven by NATO rearmament, Pacific tensions, and Middle Eastern volatility, validate its current valuation. That said, investors must recognize this is no contrarian alpha play. Instead, Northrop stock offers downside protection to portfolios (similar to gold); this trait is particularly valuable in an uncertain macroeconomic and geopolitical landscape. Despite this hedging capability, I predict only 8% upside in price over the next 12 months.

Q1 2025 Earnings Preview: Geopolitical Tailwinds Provide Protracted Upside Potential

Northrop Grumman is entering Q1 2025 on strong footing with a record backlog of $91.5 billion in orders, up 9% year-over-year. This growth reflects a wave of recent contract wins tied directly to global threat dynamics. In late 2024, Northrop secured its second low-rate initial production contract for the B-21 Raider strategic bomber and $3.5 billion deal for the U.S. Navy's E-130J "TACAMO" nuclear command-and-control aircraft. It also won a $900 million contract for an Integrated Battle Command System ('IBCS') for Poland. This is a clear example of NATO allies bolstering defenses amid the Russian-Ukraine conflict. These wins drove the company's backlog to an all-time high, providing excellent revenue visibility for 2025. In Q1, I expect to see continued conversion of this backlog into sales growth, especially in segments like Aeronautics and Defense Systems that house programs ramping up production.

The Russia-Ukraine war has been a wake-up call for NATO and Europe, spurring many nations to boost military outlays after decades of underinvestment. For example, Germany approved a special €100 billion fund to re-equip its forces, and countries across Eastern Europe (Poland, Baltic states, etc.) are purchasing new weapons systems. These trends directly benefit Northrop's portfolio. Northrop's management has noted that global geopolitical dynamics are creating new opportunities. For Q1, it's important to watch Northrop's international order flow and any commentary on European demand as a key growth driver for 2025.

Rising tensions over Taiwan are also a clear tailwind for Northrop's high-tech systems. The U.S. is now forward-deploying Northrop's MQ-4C Triton high-altitude drones to Japan to monitor Chinese forces near Taiwan. The company reported increased sales in autonomous aircraft (MQ-4C Triton and RQ-4 Global Hawk drones saw higher volumes in 2024). Northrop's B-21 Raider stealth bomber (under the company's Aeronautics division) is the key future platform for penetrating Indo-Pacific air defenses. Aeronautics sales grew 12% in 2024 driven by B-21 and F-35 work (more on F-35 in the risks section, including a discussion of drones). The Space Systems segment also stands to benefit as the U.S. invests in space-based communications, missile-warning satellites, and a modernized nuclear deterrent to counter China.

Additionally, in 2023-2024, during the Israel-Hamas war and when Iran launched missiles toward Israel amid escalating regional conflict, Northrop's share price spiked. This signals that the market expects sustained Middle East conflict would translate to greater demand for missile defense, drones, and munitions from Northrop. Northrop has an established presence in the Middle East markets for nearly 50 years. The company could also see incremental sales from countries like Israel or Gulf states enhancing their defenses against Iranian missiles and drones. However, it's apt to state that only a protracted Iran-Israel war would materially boost demand for U.S. defense firms. In Q1 2025, Middle East tensions are more of a wildcard. My base case price-target for Northrop Grumman assumes the bulk of Northrop's growth is driven by U.S. and NATO programs.

From a financial perspective, expect moderate top-line growth in Q1, consistent with full-year guidance of $42-$42.5 billion in 2025 sales. This will represent about 3% year-over-year growth for the full year. In Q1, Space Systems may still be relatively flat, but large space programs are likely to be ramping up for stronger growth from this segment in H2. Mission Systems and Defense Systems are generally high-margin businesses with stable growth. Northrop's 2025 guidance calls for EPS of $27.85-$28.25, about 7-8% growth, so Q1 will likely deliver a solid start toward that goal.

Valuation Analysis & Price Target: 7.5% 12-Month Upside

Northrop Grumman currently trades at around 19x forward non-GAAP earnings. Over the past five years, its average for the multiple is 17.5. This reveals a moderate premium on the stock price at the moment, likely as a result of geopolitical tailwinds amid increased Western-led defense measures against China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea.

The company's forward diluted EPS growth is currently 7.54%, compared to 4.09% as a five-year average. Northrop has had 15 EPS up revisions in the last three months, and only 7 down revisions. On the flip side, revenue growth has seen predominantly down revisions, and forward revenue growth is a moderate contraction from the five-year average.

As you will see in the charts below, investing in Northrop Grumman now is a little late for alpha. The forward-looking market has already recognized the increased defense spending environment. This is reflected in a 14-week RSI of 63 and the price being substantially higher than the 50-week moving average. I do not think Northrop stock will fare badly given these valuation factors, but this is certainly not a contrarian alpha play, which I am known for. Instead, I believe Northrop stock works well as a hedging mechanism at this time, much like gold.

Northrop has a TTM P/E non-GAAP ratio of 20.5. In a base case, I assume this stays steady in 12 months. My base-case TTM normalized EPS estimate is $28.55 for in 12 months, moderately above the consensus. This is because I do see defense budgets rising beyond current outlines, particularly in Europe. Therefore, I predict a $585 stock price in 12 months. This implies an 8% price growth. In other words, alpha is unlikely despite positive macro tailwinds. However, given the strategic benefits of owning the stock in portfolios amid current volatility risks, I rate the stock a Buy.

Risk Analysis: DOGE, Tension De-Escalation, & Drone/AI Competitors

A significant uncertainty currently lies in the U.S. defense spending policy. The new Department of Government Efficiency ('DOGE') aims to cut costs on major defense programs. Big-ticket projects like some of Northrop's could face scaling back or lower funding. Furthermore, if the Russia-Ukraine war cools or ends in a peace deal, the U.S. might pause supplemental defense aid. The U.S. has already halted additional Ukraine aid pending negotiations. Northrop's heavy reliance on U.S. government contracts (roughly 85% of revenue) means it is exposed to political gridlock disrupting funding. Any major de-escalation of global tensions (while beneficial to humanity) could also dampen the "fear premium" and lead to sector rotation away from defense equities.

Northrop's role in the F-35 Strike Fighter Program particularly faces headwinds as Washington targets defense budgets for cuts. Musk, the primary director of the DOGE, has openly lambasted the F-35's expense, favoring drone swarms instead. The F-35's lifetime cost is projected to exceed $2 trillion. It's not unlikely that several dozen F-35s might be sacrificed from upcoming orders to fund hundreds of autonomous weapons instead. This would directly hit Northrop Grumman. The company will need to emphasize F-35's value to national security and perhaps streamline its own production costs to weather this pressure.

The trend toward artificial intelligence in warfare and autonomous drones presents tailwinds for Anduril Industries and Shield AI, which specialize in these domains. Anduril Industries (a six-year-old startup) and General Atomics beat out Northrop, Lockheed (LMT) and Boeing (BA) in 2024 for a major Air Force contract to develop Collaborative Combat Aircraft drone wingmen. Shield AI, valued over $5 billion after recent funding, similarly is advancing AI pilots (its "Hivemind" autonomy) and teaming up with incumbents like L3Harris (LHX) on autonomous systems integration. Without proper execution moving forward, Northrop could become a legacy contractor and lose relevance in the fast-moving tech-defense era.

Northrop is aware of these challenges, which is why its stock still acts as a reliable hedge in portfolios for now. It has major drone programs such as Global Hawk and Triton. The company's expertise in advanced sensors, networks, and open-system architectures further positions it to excel in AI-driven warfare. However, as an investor, you should keep the Silicon Valley defense revolution at the forefront of your mind when considering growth moderation in traditional defense firms like Northrop. Allocate accordingly.

Conclusion: Buy

Ultimately, Northrop Grumman's investment case hinges less on rapid appreciation and more on reliability amid persistent geopolitical threats. DOGE-driven spending reviews and competition from agile AI-focused startups do pose genuine risks, but Northrop has a durable foundation to sustain it. This is not an investment to expect outsized alpha from at this time, given already-priced-in geopolitical concerns. Instead, use the stock as a portfolio hedge, similar to gold, as global conflicts continue without signs of meaningful resolution for now. Allocating to Northrop today isn't about explosive returns. It's about securing resilient performance in a world increasingly defined by geopolitical disruption.

data.title: AMLP: Midstream Is The Place To Be, Buy Rating For This Reliable ETF
data.description:
I wrote on Alerian MLP ETF (NYSEARCA:AMLP) back in early January at the start of 2025. I indicated a buy rating then, while the stock was trading around $48 per share, exactly where it is sitting today in mid-April. AMLP was up almost 10% through the first quarter this year but took a nose the first week in April. In the professional golf world, even-par isn’t usually good enough, but for AMLP, even-par this year doesn’t sound too bad in this rocky market.

AMLP is an exchange-traded fund that gives exposure to energy infrastructure Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs) that earn most of their cash flow from midstream activities across energy, transportation, storage, and processing in the energy sector. The ALPS Advisors fund began in 2010 and is capitalization weighted.

I still rate AMLP a buy. Tariffs are adding a twist to the oil scene, but I still think energy is a good sector to invest in right now. It is a relatively recession resistant sector, and midstream is the segment to be in. The top holdings in AMLP have a great outlook, and outside of appreciation, the dividend is tremendous. Upstream and downstream will be more affected by the economic and political picture, leaving AMLP as an ideal candidate in this cumbersome time.

Internal

Since I last wrote the top holdings have shuffled around slightly by weight, but the top six are still intact and make up about 71% of the portfolio. AMLP only has 14 holdings now in which about half come from pipeline transportation of either petroleum or natural gas. The other half comes from the gathering, processing and distribution of oil industries.

The current top four in weight are Sunoco LP Common Units (SUN), Western Midstream Partners LP Common Units (WES), MPLX LP Common Units (MPLX), and Energy Transfer LP Common Units (ET). Quant and analyst ratings are nearly a consistent buy across the board for these four.

These stocks have all outpaced the S&P 500, which is down almost 10% over the last 6 months. WES has dropped 5% and is the only gathering and processing stock of the group. The others have all gained between 3 and 13% in this period. All four are fairly valued with P/E ratios between 9.40 and 13.32 for the bunch. The heavyweights in AMLP have recently been strong and have momentum on their side moving forward.

Upstream vs Midstream vs Downstream

The price of oil is naturally volatile during uncertain economic periods, as it is traded more on speculation and demand is often reduced. From a United States and International perspective, the world seems to be as uncertain as it has in a long time. On the other hand, like we know, energy will always be needed and is a somewhat secure sector. Midstream is the perfect fit amidst it all, being much less tied to the price of oil.

For comparison’s sake, I am using iShares U.S. Oil & Gas Exploration and Production ETF (IEO), SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Exploration and Production ETF (XOP) for upstream, and VanEck Oil Refiners ETF (CRAK) as downstream. The charts above perfectly showcase specifically how linked upstream is to oil price. In the summer of 2022, Crude Oil WTI hit its peak at about $118 a barrel. At the same time, both IEO and XOP were at their top and began the decline simultaneously with the oil price. Oil price has dropped slowly since April 2024, all three peers have seen a decline since then, while AMLP has seen gains.

One more short-term look. When the price of oil got crushed the first week of April this month, AMLP was much less affected than the other three ETFs. An argument against AMLP would be its higher expense ratio, which is 0.85%. The other three range from 0.35-0.62%, so at worse AMLP is half a percent more expensive.

The cost to buy AMLP is the only negative outlier. It is currently graded much better on all other accounts. Momentum is obvious, as midstream has been doing fine through the oil volatility. From a liquidity standpoint, AMLP is only behind XOP in average daily share volume.

The biggest win, and the most underrated part of AMLP is its dividend. A 7.88% yield provides an almost incomparable passive side to this investment. CRAK has a 5.94% yield, and the other two are both under 3%. AMLP and midstream are better suited for the current environment right now.

Midstream Competitor

We've gone through the internal companies, and the upstream/downstream comparison, now let’s see how AMLP stacks up against a midstream competitor in Global X MLP Energy & Infrastructure ETF (MLPX). Over the last 5 years, MLPX has outgained AMLP by 32%. AMLP is more expensive, but MLPX is less liquid. MLPX has a higher current valuation with a P/E at 18.59, whereas AMLP is at 14.61. I think both are a good investment, but the separator for me is the dividend for AMLP which is 3.25% higher.

Conclusion

I really like the midstream industry right now. Energy is a fine place to be for investors, and midstream is much more reliable than upstream or downstream due to the latter being bound to oil price movement. The upcoming direction for the top holdings of AMLP looks strong. Take it easy in this challenging market and buy AMLP for its remarkable dividend and safe outlook toward price gains.

data.title: Genuine Parts: Potential Winners For Tariff Protection, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Here at the Lab, we are upgrading Genuine Parts (NYSE:GPC) to a strong buy recommendation. From a tariff perspective, we view the company as one of the better-insulated players in our auto coverage, with the potential for earnings to increase. As a reminder, we have a long-standing buy rating on GPC backed by 1) an aging car population, 2) increasing car complexity, and 3) a lower valuation compared to peers, combined with supportive capital allocation priorities (GPC is a dividend aristocrats players), In our last analysis, we also reported positive evidence from Advance Auto's withdrawal from key markets. This might present further sales opportunities for Genuine Parts. Following our Q4 results comment and an additional -6.85% in stock price decline (Fig 1), we believe GPC is a solid buy.

Fig 1

Why are we positive?

  1. We believe the market is not realizing that we still have 10% baseline tariffs on all countries globally and 25% on auto imports to the USA. Additionally, with a direct impact on the auto industry, tariffs were also unchanged on steel, aluminum, and Mexico (with base tariffs of 25%). This is also a 90-day pause and does not temporarily end the trade war. Many analysts expected higher vehicle costs of $5,000 to $10,000. Despite the final numbers, this very well supports our investment thesis. New car purchases are less attractive to consumers. Thus, consumers will likely delay new car purchases and invest in maintaining their current vehicles, increasing the demand for auto parts. 63% of GPC's top-line sales come from replacement auto parts, so the company is well-positioned to weather the tariff impact positively. There is also another consideration, based on our expectation, that used vehicles are likely to cost more. Therefore, there is more value in repairing the car you do have than extending its useful life. As a reminder, the company will play offense concerning capturing share losses from Advance Auto, as the closed stores should result in the migration of independents from the CarQuest network to NAPA (GPC's latest acquisition);
  2. Additionally, 37% of GPC's sales are related to Industrial activities. Once again, to the extent that manufacturing moves back to the US, onshoring industrial demand would likely benefit the GPC segment (which decreased by 1.4% and 4% in 2024, on sales and EBITDA, respectively);
  3. Historically, replacement parts - especially in auto - are well positioned to pass on higher input costs and defend margin. From a tariff perspective, we believe GPC can pass through rising prices in both segments, potentially increasing earnings. This is also supported by restructuring actions to cost structure reduction. In our numbers, we project saving $100 million in 2025 with an EBIT step-up of at least 40 basis points. The GPC combined effect of 2024 and 2025 cost savings is expected to reach >$200 million in annualized savings by 2026. The company will also invest in new, more efficient HR and payroll technologies. On a positive note, the company is partnering with Google on an AI-driven search and catalog maintenance enhancement. This will translate into efficiency and savings on time and labor (with lower headcount). This is key to consider, given the fact that people make up approximately 65% of the company’s SG&A expense;
  4. Aside from pricing power and the positive upside of the aging car population, the company is protected from COGS purchases. 7% of the company’s purchases come from APAC (China), and less than 5% come from Canada & Mexico;
  5. Wall Street analysts are pessimistic about GPC so that the companies might be surprised by the upside. We think GPC's risks are associated with a choppy low-income consumer, but we believe there is minimal negative elasticity on replacement auto parts. Compared to the past, GPC offers a measure of tariff protection at an attractive 3-4x P/E discount compared to the recent past (Fig 2). Sentiment is mainly negative, and we think tariff-related pricing power, accretive M&A deals, and improvement from industrial offer a compelling risk-reward. Regarding downside protection, GPC remains committed to its existing capital allocation policies, favoring a DPS increase year-on-year.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Our last assessment projected 2025 sales of plus 2% to $24 billion. This was based on FX pressure, positive M&A acquisitions, and Advance Auto's withdrawal from key USA markets. On the gross margin, we positively reported 36.9% in Q4 2024 with a plus 50 basis points year-on-year growth, which was also ahead of consensus. Given our positive expectations on pricing power and the continuous delicate momentum of the aging car population, we believe GPC could land at the high end of the gross margin guide with an expansion of at least 50 basis points. Incorporating cost savings initiated, our core operating profit might move from 6.6% to 6.8%. This implied a 2025 EBIT of $1.63 billion. Regarding Q1, we expect lower earnings due to one less selling day, higher depreciation, and a negative impact on the US dollar. So, in Q1, we are modeling an EPS of $1.67. On a yearly basis, without M&A, and a FCF supported by debt reduction with a 2025 interest payment of $130 million, we arrived at a profit before tax of $1.49 billion. Applying a 24% tax rate (aligned with GPC's historical corporate tax rate), our net income is estimated at $1.15 billion with an EPS of $8.2. This is slightly above our previous EPS expectation of $8.1.

GPC's valuation is compelling, and considering the dividend and cash flow generation, this adds a supportive view. The company trades at a P/E of 13.9x, a 3-4x discount to its five-year average (Fig 2). Relative to the S&P 500, historically, GPC usually gets a 10-15% discount, but now receives a 30% P/E discount. This is also true on the FWD EV/EBITDA basis (10x vs 12-13x). We believe the company offers tariff protection measures with an attractive valuation discount. The sentiment is negative, so we might have a Wall Street upside over the year. Valuing the company with an unchanged P/E multiple target of 16x, we increase our target to $131.1.

Fig 2

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) lower-than-forecasted earnings growth from tariffs and industrial recovery, 2) failure in accretive acquisitions, 3) lower-than-expected cost savings optimization, 4) declines in the average age of vehicles or miles driven in the EU and USA, 5) online channel shift, 6) higher inventory as reported in Q4 2024, and 7) change in FX (26% of GPC sales are not denominated in US dollars: 16% in EUR, and 10% in Australian Dollar.

Conclusion

Our positive overweight rating is backed by supportive expectations related to tariffs. Additionally, there is an onshoring play on the industrial segment. With the new management team helping the company towards a shift in IT, supply chain, and process improvement, we believe the company will likely increase its margin. Combined with an attractive valuation relative to its peers, GPC is a buy on a 12-month view. Additionally, the company screens dividend coverage and balance sheet ratios well.

data.title: JEPQ: High-Yield Plus Tech Exposure
data.description:
The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ) is a tech-oriented ETF that is also heavily concentrated in ‘magnificent 7’ stocks. With recent market turbulence affecting tech stocks and tech-focused ETF’s, especially heavily, I believe the valuation draw-down constitutes a buying opportunity for investors. The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF also employs a call writing strategy, meaning the ETF leverages its long position in magnificent 7 equities to generate option income that is paid to investors in the form of a monthly dividend. Recently, the ETF's yield has surged above to 11.8%, indicating undervaluation.

Previous rating

In my last work on the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF -- Earn 9.5% For Owning Tech Stocks -- I indicated that the ETF was an attractive investment option for investors that want to maintain a long position in leading U.S. technology companies and at the same time generate income from the ETF selling covered calls. The sell-off in technology stocks, led by some of the largest tech companies in the world, creates a golden buying opportunity for investors that focus on long term investing goals, in my opinion, which is why I am confirming my buy rating for JEQP.

Tech-heavy, mag-7 investment portfolio for income investors

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF is focused on investing in leading U.S. technology companies, which is why firms like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA) represent the top three holdings of the ETF with weightings of 7.2%, 6.9% and 6.7%. These three tech companies represent about half of the ETF’s top ten holdings, making them absolutely critical to the performance of the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF going forward.

Other magnificent 7 stocks, which are the leading U.S. tech enterprises in the Cloud, social media, e-Commerce and electric vehicle markets, are also all included in the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF’s portfolio, including Alphabet (GOOG), Tesla (TSLA), Meta Platforms (META) and Amazon (AMZN). All of these companies benefit from growing AI adoption and all of them are leaders in their respective industries.

Further, with AI spending set to ramp up massively in FY 2025 and beyond, I believe the AI theme is going to be the most dominant investment theme for years to come. For this reason, I believe that the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF is a top investment option for long term investors. I have also made the same argument recently about the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), a tech-oriented ETF: I Have Been Waiting For A Drop Like This.

This is what the top ten of JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF's portfolio looks like right now...

A main alternative to the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF is the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) which is not focused on the tech sector, but much more broadly positioned. The main difference between these two covered call ETFs therefore is that the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF offers a better (defensive) portfolio positioning while the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF is aggressively focused on tech stocks.

Both ETFs have the same (0.35%) net expense ratio, but major differences can be found in the size of the ETF portfolios as well as in the length of the performance period. The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF manages $22.2B, while the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF has an about 67% larger investment portfolio: it manages $37.1B.

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF was launched on May 3, 2022 while the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF has a two-year longer performance history: it was launched on May 20, 2020.

In addition to owning equity positions in the portfolio, the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF writes (sells) out-of-the-money Nasdaq 100 Index call options for premium income that is distributed to shareholders in the form of a monthly distribution. Both JEPQ's and JEPI's dividend yields have surged on the back of the panic that materialized in the market after the president announced new tariffs, with the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF now paying an almost 12% yield.

Performance overview

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF has achieved an annual average NAV return of 12.64% since inception, which compares against an NAV return, also since inception, of 11.84% for the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF. I like both JEPQ and JEPI, but have a slight preference for the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF due to its aggressive exposure to magnificent 7 stocks.

JEPQ compared against JEPI

Both ETFs have achieved double-digit annual net asset value growth since their inception dates, but both ETFs have not been around for very long which is why investors, in my opinion, should not place too much weight on their performance histories alone. Year-to-date, both ETFs have suffered declines in their net asset values with JEPQ, due to its aggressive tech positioning, losing about twice as much value as JEPI. Given that my long term outlook for tech stocks is very positive, given increasing corporate spending on generative AI, I believe the valuation draw-down is a buying opportunity for investors.

Risks with JEPQ

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF is focused heavily on magnificent 7 as well as U.S. tech stocks, which makes the ETF a highly concentrated investment which could become a problem if the technology sector were to underperform going forward. On the other hand, technology has performed very well in the last decade and if this trend continues, the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF has massive out-performance potential long term. I believe the risk profile favors the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF, but investors that are concerned with the attractiveness of the U.S. tech sector or that want to be better diversified, may want to consider the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF instead.

Final thoughts

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF is an attractive investment option for investors that want to combine a high 12% dividend yield with a long position in the leading tech franchises in the U.S. The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF’s portfolio includes all magnificent 7 stocks, all of which have exposure to AI in one form or another. In my opinion, the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF is a top investment choice for investors that want to overweight technology stocks, and magnificent 7 stocks especially. The combination of tech exposure with a high yield translates to attractive long term income potential for investors that are willing (and capable) to sit out potentially longer periods of high market volatility.
